content.problemStatement:
You are given two positive integers NNN and KKK.

A permutation P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ of the first NNN positive integers is good if the following statement holds:


Consider a set SSS of segments, initially empty. For each iii (1≤i≤N−11 \leq i \leq N-11≤i≤N−1) such that Pi<Pi+1P_i \lt P_{i+1}Pi​<Pi+1​, insert the segment [Pi,Pi+1][P_i, P_{i+1}][Pi​,Pi+1​] into SSS. Then, if you pick any subset of KKK segments of SSS, their intersection is empty.
Count the number of good permutations P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2,\dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​. As the result can be very large, you should print it modulo 998 244 353998\,244\,353998244353.


Input Format
The only line contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.



Output Format
Print a single integer: the number of good permutations, modulo 998 244 353998\,244\,353998244353.
content.inputFormat: The input consists of a single line containing two integers N and K (2 ≤ N ≤ 2000, 1 ≤ K ≤ N‑1).
content.outputFormat: Print a single integer – the number of good permutations modulo 998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Only the decreasing permutation has no ascents, therefore S is empty and the condition holds.
There are 2820 permutations where no cut is crossed by 3 or more ascents.
Out of 24 permutations, 13 satisfy the depth ≤ 1 condition.

solution.approach: The problem can be transformed into counting permutations where for every cut between consecutive values x and x+1 the number of increasing adjacent pairs (ascents) that cross the cut is at most K‑1.  An ascent (P_i, P_{i+1}) with P_i < P_{i+1} crosses exactly the cuts x with P_i ≤ x < P_{i+1}.  Therefore we need to count permutations such that the load on each cut never exceeds K‑1.  This can be solved with a dynamic programming over the set of values processed so far.  While inserting the next value v we keep track of how many ascents currently cross each cut.  The state can be compressed because the load on a cut depends only on the relative order of the already placed values.  The DP runs in O(N·K) time and O(K) memory.  The final answer is the sum of DP states after all N values have been placed.  All operations are performed modulo 998244353.
